a nice way to check coverage without waiting for workflows, with genhtml and lcov, in the case of rust I use
```bash
cargo llvm-cov --lcov -- --nocapture --color=always > ./coverage.lcov 2>/dev/null
genhtml -o coverage-html ./coverage.lcov
cd coverage-html
python3 -m http.server 8000
```

TIL - Rust has a sexy Compile time warning system:

```rust
warning: unused `Result` that must be used
 --> src/main.rs:9:5
  |
9 |     io::stdin().read_line(&mut guess);
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |
  = note: this `Result` may be an `Err` variant, which should be handled
  = note: `#[warn(unused_must_use)]` on by default
help: use `let _ = ...` to ignore the resulting value
  |
9 |     let _ = io::stdin().read_line(&mut guess);
  |     +++++++

warning: `guessing_game` (bin "guessing_game") generated 1 warning
    Finished `dev` profile [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.23s
```

TIL x86 is a whole family and its a mess
its a part of 8088(8bit) and first one was 8086 (16bit) then they continued this naming scheme like 80186, 80286, 80386 (32bit) but the code names were something iAP 186 wher 1 is generation and rest is 86 so people commonly ref to them as x86 but every generation is kinda different and code names remains a mess

TIL:
there is a Unicode consortium founded in 1991 , that is in-charge for the char on internet and devices across the world. they publishes new Unicode version which includes , new code points, character names, properties (is it a letter, emoji, punctuation, etc.) , then os vendor and language runtimes pick it up. this is so cool. and shows if done right software never dies.

messages.content:
I have finally completed the Version 1 of my Terminal based ASCII Utility tool. 
Since the time I started learning Python, I have liked the ways we can translate ASCII into images, videos, etc. 
So it was on my bucket list to make a Terminal based Utility tool with nice logs, and smart ways to save and read files so that I can make it Open Source and all, So that I will have nicely rendered ASCII images and methods to put them into various things, like websites, some random posts on Discord or YT. An entire 4K wallpaper scaled down and transformed using only Numpy only. I finally have jumped straight into the docs to figure out and learn on the go about PIL (to turn it into frames) and OpenCV (only for Webcam feed and Videos), the 2 out of the holy 3 for building TUI tools, (3rd one being PyGames, of course). 

I have never written tests for any projects yet, but as the code-base is big and about to be expand a lot from Open Source Contributions, I will clean things up → Write `readme.md` files → Explaining the rough calculations and processes → Assumptions made around the code and function calls → Naming conventions → Explain things using Video walkthroughs → Finding a nice way to document things → Test things up, and then I will put it into GitHub, by the end of February 2026 (I have started thinking and building small scripts around it from 3rd of February 2026)  as I have mid-sems starting from next week. I have completed it.

Even if no one would use it, I will. As in I have made this tool keeping me in my mind. And hence this is the V1 of ASCII-Utilities
Code: arr[i][j] = ord(pic(i][j])

No, U need to make it user-friendly & optimized, **examples** ⬇️ :
1. U need to find way to load a frame, or a video or an image on to an external window
2. Gray-scale and shift the channels, trim them so that u have only Green part of the content in it, and other channels are thrown away for now, as eyes can find the green color faster among all other channels + I don't have knowledge to mix and match layers. Find a way to store the ARRAY NOW, the image has been decomposed into a Numpy array now.
3. Tailor to terminal windows, so it retains the actual frame's characteristics that makes the image "the image", on top of that u need to break the image into chunks of a size such that it dynamically adjusts itself to the proper terminal width and breadth even if u resize the window and run the code again, making the code work across any terminal instance. And then once chunks are built u have to take an average of each chunk and then assign an ASCII character following below ↓↓
4. Then u have to cherry picked brightness specific sequence for the ASCII characters which are printable on the terminal natively on Python, one that I used was, ↓↓
`$@B%8&WM#*oahkbdpqwmZO0QLCJUYXzcvunxrjft/\|()1{}[]?-_+~<>i!lI;:,\"^'. `
5. U have to fine-tune the mapping from raw array to the above characters while maintaining enough spacing for terminal and paced to make it fast for videos, so that a certain long video would be processed in a certain time.
6. If the video is too big, u have to cut the video into 2 - 3 mins long chunks, name them, loop through them, And then use multi-threading to build them on parallel on each channel, convert them individually into `ascii_video_part_n`. Then stitch them back together in order.
7. For the live web cam feed, u have to do a whole other levels of optimizations.

messages.content:
I have no words, try to run these programs!

```py
a = 100
b = 100
print(a is b) # true
a = 300
b = 300
print(a is b) # false
```

```py
import io
from contextlib import redirect_stdout

buf = io.StringIO()
with redirect_stdout(buf):
  import this

output = buf.getvalue()
print(output[456:-265])
```
idk what the second one does but first happens because small numbers like smaller than 256 (iirc) get cached and reused right?
yes, in cpython (the most popular implementation)

Ye @Kzeckt  it also is not just a cpython thing
Just try to run the second one, it's prints out sone string that is not in the program

It isn't exclusive to that ig, it is more like an implementation optimization, not something fixed for the language, i.e. might differ depending on the implementation. CPython is just one which definitely has this. And I didn't run the second one.
Today I stole 50 pamphlets from the advertisement guys.
just why

Yeah, it's not exclusive to that, like Pydantic also does this and so on, also i give up gatekeeping the output. Basically, it prints out
```
There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.
Although that way may not be obvious at first unless you're Dutch.
```

This is a line from the `Zen of Python` poem, which is printed to stdout when the `this` frozen module is imported (fun fact, the `this` module has a frozen function that is a rot13 cypher)
